subject: mathematics

sections.title: Perimeter and Area: A Recap
sections.think_reflect: Whether two figures with the same perimeter must also have the same area, and why this need not be true.

sections.title: Area of a Triangle Using Base and Height
sections.think_reflect: Why the formula for the area of a triangle still works when the foot of the altitude lies outside the base.

sections.title: Heron's Formula
sections.think_reflect: Why Heron's formula gives the same area as the base-height method even though it uses only the three side lengths.

sections.title: Application of Heron's Formula in Finding Areas of Quadrilaterals
Brahmagupta's Formula Generalises Heron's Formula
sections.think_reflect: Whether Brahmagupta's formula for a cyclic quadrilateral reduces correctly to Heron's formula when one side shrinks to zero.

sections.title: Visualising and Calculating Areas of Pathways (Border, Crossroads) in Rectangular Shapes
Areas of Combination of Plane Figures

prose_summary: Section 6.1 introduces the chapter's purpose: extending earlier knowledge of perimeter and area to new methods and shapes encountered in everyday measurement problems, particularly cases where the height of a figure is not directly given. Section 6.2 recaps the standard perimeter and area formulas for rectangles, squares, parallelograms, and triangles built up in earlier grades, and explains that perimeter and area are independent measures, so figures of equal perimeter can have unequal area and figures of equal area can have unequal perimeter. Section 6.3 explains the area of a triangle using base and height, addressing the case where the altitude from a vertex falls outside the triangle's base when the triangle is obtuse, and justifies why the area formula remains valid by extending the base and treating the area as a difference of two right-triangle areas. Section 6.4 derives Heron's formula for the area of a triangle from its three side lengths alone, introduces the semi-perimeter notation, and proves through worked computation that this formula agrees with the base-height formula, making it useful when the height is unknown or difficult to measure directly. Section 6.5 explains how Heron's formula extends to find the area of quadrilaterals by splitting them into two triangles along a diagonal, computing each triangle's area separately, and summing the results, including the special case of an isosceles trapezium. Section 6.6 narrates a historical episode about the mathematician Brahmagupta's discovery of a generalised area formula, framing the motivation for the next section's result. Section 6.7 derives and justifies Brahmagupta's formula for the area of a cyclic quadrilateral in terms of its four sides and semi-perimeter, and shows algebraically that the formula reduces to Heron's formula when one side length is set to zero, thereby generalising the triangle case. Section 6.8 explains and computes the areas of pathways such as borders running around the inside or outside of a rectangular field and crossroads running through the middle of a rectangular plot, decomposing each pathway into rectangles and adjusting for overlapping regions where paths intersect. Section 6.9 explains and computes the areas enclosed between two concentric circular paths (circular borders or rings), using the difference of the two circles' areas, and solves problems involving radius, width of path, and material cost based on area. Section 6.10 explains and computes the combined area of plane figures built by joining or overlapping simple shapes such as rectangles, triangles, and semicircles, decomposing each composite figure into known shapes whose areas are added or subtracted appropriately.

enumerated_worked_examples.description: Finds the area enclosed between two intersecting circles of given radii by decomposing the overlapping region.

Shows that two different paths around a rectangular park (a straight diagonal path versus a path along two sides) need not enclose equal areas even when related by the same boundary lengths, addressing the perimeter-versus-area distinction.
Computes the area of an equilateral triangle of given side length using Heron's formula and verifies the result.
Computes the area of an isosceles triangle using Heron's formula.
Computes the area of a 3-4-5 right triangle using Heron's formula and confirms it matches the base-height computation.
Uses Brahmagupta's formula to find the area of a cyclic rectangle and checks the result against the standard rectangle area formula.
Uses Brahmagupta's formula to find the area of an isosceles trapezium inscribed in a circle.

enumerated_exercises.description: Finds the area of a triangle given its base and corresponding height.
Finds the area of a parallelogram given base and height.
Compares the perimeters and areas of two given rectangular figures.
Finds the area of a figure composed of a rectangle and triangle combined.
Determines whether two figures with equal perimeter have equal area, with justification.
Finds the area of a given irregular plane figure by decomposition into known shapes.
Solves a word problem involving area of a rectangular plot and cost of levelling.
Solves a word problem on the area of a triangular field given two sides and an included measurement.
Finds the area of a triangle with given three side lengths using Heron's formula.
Finds the area of an isosceles triangle with given equal sides and base using Heron's formula.
Finds the area of a scalene triangle with given sides using Heron's formula.
Finds the perimeter of a triangle given its area and two sides, working backward from Heron's formula.
Solves a word problem on the area of a triangular park using Heron's formula.
Finds the area of an equilateral triangle given its side length, deriving the general formula via Heron's formula.
Solves a word problem comparing the cost of fencing or levelling triangular plots using Heron's formula.
Finds the area of a triangle whose sides are in a given ratio and whose perimeter is known.
Finds the area of an isosceles triangle given its perimeter and base.
Solves a word problem involving area of a triangular signboard or similar object using Heron's formula.
Finds the area of a quadrilateral field by splitting it into two triangles and applying Heron's formula to each.
Finds the area of a cyclic quadrilateral with given four sides using Brahmagupta's formula.
Finds the area of a quadrilateral field by decomposing it into two triangles using a diagonal.
Finds the area of an isosceles trapezium with given parallel sides and legs.
Solves a word problem on the area of a quadrilateral-shaped plot using diagonal decomposition.
Verifies that Brahmagupta's formula reduces to Heron's formula for a degenerate quadrilateral with one side equal to zero.
Finds the area of a rhombus-shaped field given its side and one diagonal, using triangle decomposition.
Finds the width of a uniform border around a rectangular lawn given the area of the border.
Finds the area of two crossroads of given width running through the middle of a rectangular park, accounting for the overlapping square.
Finds the area of a circular path of given width running around a circular garden.
Solves a word problem on the cost of constructing or paving a circular path around a circular plot using area.
Finds the area of a combined plane figure made of a rectangle and a semicircle joined along one side.
Finds the area of a composite figure formed by combining a square and a triangle.
Finds the area of a plot shaped as a combination of a rectangle and triangle using given measurements.
Finds the area of a decorative or floor-design figure formed by combining multiple plane shapes.
Solves a word problem requiring the total area of a composite figure to compute material cost or quantity.
